skill.name: command-creator
skill.description: Guides users through creating Claude Code slash commands with clear structure, workflow patterns, argument handling, and reusable agent instructions.
skill.summary: Create reusable Claude Code slash commands for repeatable developer workflows.
skill.author: softaworks

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged many shell, filesystem, weak-crypto, and reconnaissance patterns, but review shows they are markdown guidance and example slash-command content rather than executable package code. The real risk is medium because the skill helps create persistent Claude Code commands that may run shell tools, edit repositories, and submit PRs when a generated command is invoked. No evidence found of prompt injection, credential exfiltration, hidden executable payloads, or malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Generated Commands Can Run Shell Workflows
medium_findings.description: The skill teaches generated slash commands to use shell-oriented tools for tests, formatters, git, make, and Graphite. This is legitimate for developer automation, but a generated command could modify code, commit changes, or publish PRs if the user accepts that workflow.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The shell commands are documented examples and agent instructions, not code that runs during skill loading. Risk remains because the skill intentionally creates commands that can later execute these workflows.

medium_findings.title: Persistent Slash Command File Creation
medium_findings.description: The skill creates markdown command files under project or user Claude command directories. This is expected behavior, but global commands persist across projects and can influence future Claude Code sessions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The persistence behavior is explicit and central to the skill purpose. The risk is bounded because files are command markdown created through the normal Claude Code command mechanism.

low_findings.title: Static High-Risk Hits Are Mostly Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-crypto, reconnaissance, and backtick findings occur inside markdown explanations, templates, and examples. I did not find evidence that these strings are active malware or hidden executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected locations are prose or fenced markdown examples for command design. The scanner patterns are real strings, but their semantic context does not show direct execution by the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Examples in Generated Workflows
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples include git, make, test, formatter, and Graphite commands. These commands are normal for development automation but can change repository state when copied into a generated command.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are plainly visible in markdown examples and match the skill purpose. They are risky only after a user creates and invokes a command based on them.

dangerous_patterns.title: Writes to Claude Command Directories
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs creation of command files in project and user command directories. User-level commands can be reused globally, so generated content should be reviewed before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file-write target is documented directly in the workflow. It is not malicious, but persistence is an important marketplace risk factor.

content.user_title: Create Reusable Claude Code Slash Commands
content.value_statement: Teams repeat complex developer workflows and lose consistency across sessions. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to create reusable slash commands with clear steps and safer boundaries.
actual_capabilities: Chooses project-level or user-level command locations based on repository context.
Explains common slash command patterns for workflow automation, CI fixing, delegation, and tool wrappers.
Collects command names, arguments, workflow steps, tool guidance, and success criteria.
Generates command markdown with frontmatter, instructions, examples, and error handling.
Uses bundled references for patterns, complete examples, and quality checks.
Supports iteration after users test a generated command.
limitations: It creates guidance and command files, not a standalone command runner.
Generated commands may run shell tools only when invoked by the user.
It depends on Claude Code command directory conventions.
It does not validate every generated command against project security policy.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Workflows
use_cases.description: Create project commands for repeated review, testing, release, or PR workflows that teammates can reuse.

use_cases.title: Automate Personal Developer Tasks
use_cases.description: Turn frequent local tasks into global Claude Code commands available across repositories.

use_cases.title: Document Agent Procedures
use_cases.description: Convert multi-step agent procedures into clear slash commands with arguments, tool rules, and stop conditions.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Command
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Claude Code slash command for running our project tests and reporting failures clearly.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Command With Arguments
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a slash command that accepts a base branch argument and reviews changes against that branch.

prompt_templates.title: Create an Iterative Fix Command
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a command that runs CI, fixes common failures, verifies again, and stops when repeated errors remain.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Delegated Planning Command
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a slash command that gathers context, delegates planning to a specialist agent, and saves the plan only after approval.

output_examples.input: Create a command for running CI until it passes.
output_examples.output: A command plan with a concise name, command location, iterative CI steps, stuck detection, and final reporting guidance.

output_examples.input: Make a global command for reviewing a branch.
output_examples.output: A reusable user-level command design with an optional base branch argument and clear review output expectations.

output_examples.input: Turn our release checklist into a slash command.
output_examples.output: A structured workflow command with ordered release steps, validation checks, error handling, and usage instructions.

best_practices: Review generated commands before saving them globally because global commands affect future projects.
Use explicit success criteria, stop conditions, and error handling in every command.
Keep command names kebab-case and descriptions concise for clear help output.
anti_patterns: Do not create broad commands that run destructive shell actions without confirmation.
Do not store secrets, tokens, or private operational details inside command files.
Do not rely on vague instructions such as fix everything or check the project.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It creates Claude Code slash command markdown files for reusable agent workflows.

faq.question: Can it create project-specific commands?
faq.answer: Yes. It can place commands under the project command directory when the workflow belongs to one repository.

faq.question: Can it create global commands?
faq.answer: Yes. It can create user-level commands when the workflow should be available across repositories.

faq.question: Does it execute the generated command automatically?
faq.answer: No. It creates the command and may suggest testing it after user review.

faq.question: Why is the security rating medium?
faq.answer: Generated commands can run shell tools and persist across projects, so users should review them before use.

faq.question: Which tools does it support?
faq.answer: The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code marketplace contexts.
